coredata.dc:title: Recent advances in molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) for visual recognition and inhibition of α-dicarbonyl compound-mediated Maillard reaction products

coredata.dc:description: α-Dicarbonyl compounds (α-DCs) are important intermediates and precursors of harmful Maillard reaction products (e.g., acrylamide and late glycosylation end-products), and they exist widely in thermoprocessed sugar- or fat-rich foods. α-DCs and their end-products are prone to accumulation in the human body and lead to the development of various chronic diseases. Therefore, detection of α-DCs and their associated hazards in food samples is crucial. This paper reviews the preparation of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) enabling visual intelligent responses and the strategies for recognition and capture of α-DCs and their associated hazards, and provides a comprehensive summary of the development of visual MIPs, including integration strategies and applications with real food samples. The visual signal responses as well as the mechanisms for hazard recognition and capture are highlighted. Current challenges and prospects for visual MIPs with advanced applications in food, agricultural and environmental samples are also discussed. This review will open new horizons regarding visual MIPs for recognition and inhibition of hazards in food safety.

(f) Fluorescence intensity change of CDs@Eu-MOFs after the addition of Hg2+ and the colour change under a 365 nm UV lamp (Guo et al., 2021b). head.ce:title: Recent advances in molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) for visual recognition and inhibition of α-dicarbonyl compound-mediated Maillard reaction products

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food and Health, Beijing Technology and Business University (BTBU), No. 11 Fucheng Road, Beijing 100048, People’s Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food and Health, Beijing Technology and Business University (BTBU), No. 11 Fucheng Road, Beijing 100048, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: α-Dicarbonyl compounds (α-DCs) are important intermediates and precursors of harmful Maillard reaction products (e.g., acrylamide and late glycosylation end-products), and they exist widely in thermoprocessed sugar- or fat-rich foods. α-DCs and their end-products are prone to accumulation in the human body and lead to the development of various chronic diseases. Therefore, detection of α-DCs and their associated hazards in food samples is crucial. This paper reviews the preparation of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) enabling visual intelligent responses and the strategies for recognition and capture of α-DCs and their associated hazards, and provides a comprehensive summary of the development of visual MIPs, including integration strategies and applications with real food samples. The visual signal responses as well as the mechanisms for hazard recognition and capture are highlighted. Current challenges and prospects for visual MIPs with advanced applications in food, agricultural and environmental samples are also discussed. This review will open new horizons regarding visual MIPs for recognition and inhibition of hazards in food safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Most plant-based and animal-based foods require thermal treatments, including high- and low-temperature steaming, sterilization, ultraviolet irradiation, and pasteurization, to preserve the food, extend its shelf life, and ensure safe consumption. However, the Maillard reaction occurs during the thermal treatments of foods, and according to an investigation by the Food and Drug Administration reported in 2021, 126 potentially harmful substances are produced during Maillard reactions (Törős et al., 2023). α-Dicarbonyl compounds (α-DCs) are important intermediates and precursors of harmful Maillard reaction products, such as acrylamide (AM) and advanced glycosylation end products (AGEs), and exist in many thermoprocessed sugar- or fat-rich foods (Zhang et al., 2020a). There are many kinds of α-DCs, including 3-deoxyglucone (3-DG), glyoxal (GO), methylglyoxal (MGO) and diacetyl (DA) (Qi et al., 2023). α-DCs affect food quality and serve as potential biomarkers for oxidative stress-related diseases through metabolic pathological effects (El-Maghrabey et al., 2018). Therefore, recognition and inhibition of α-DCs is important for evaluating food safety and quality, and for monitoring diseases in the early stages.

Targeted analyses and precise control are crucial for the processes of recognition and inhibition (Zhang et al., 2023). Especially in complex food matrices, it is necessary to ensure the internal nutritional composition while controlling the growth and reproduction of harmful substances, and the most important goal is to avoid introducing pollutants from external sources. Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) are synthetic analogues of natural biological antibody-antigen systems that selectively bind to template molecules during syntheses through the “lock and key” mechanism (Ostovan et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2023a). Usually, the syntheses of MIPs require prepolymerization with template molecules, followed by the formation of recognition cavities that complement the shapes, sizes, or spatial structures of the template molecules in a highly cross-linked polymer matrix via elution (Yuan et al., 2023a; Zhang et al., 2023; Zhou et al., 2019). Therefore, MIPs exhibit good recombination selectivities and are easy to identify. To add “eyes” to traditional MIPs so that they can direct adsorption-reading, it is important to add visual response elements during polymerization. Common photoluminescent nanoelements include quantum dots (QDs), carbon dots (CDs), peptide dots (PDs), metal nanoparticles (MNs), metal organic frameworks (MOFs), and covalent organic frameworks (COFs). Interestingly, these luminescent centres may emit one, two or multiple peaks, giving MIPs multiple colours and more three-dimensional luminescence.

In this review, we comprehensively discuss recent advances in the use of MIPs for visual recognition and inhibition of α-DC-mediated thermal processing. Here, we highlight recent developments in visual MIPs, including integration strategies and applications in the recognition and inhibition of α-DCs and their associated hazards. Furthermore, we explore and discuss the mechanism for molecular recognition with α-DCs and visual MIPs in detail. Finally, the current challenges and prospects for visual MIPs are also discussed to support their uses in food, agricultural and environmental samples.

Due to the lack of signal responses, traditional MIPs require the use of techniques with large instruments, such as gas chromatography and liquid chromatography, to read the adsorbed data (Zhou et al., 2018; Hou et al., 2021). These large instruments are expensive and complicated to operate. However, with the inclusion of visual response elements, visual MIPs can avoid this difficult problem. Currently, visual MIP structures are mainly classified asembedded structures, core–shell structures, hollow-shell structures, or mesoporous structures (Yang et al., 2018) (Fig. 1 ).

MIPs with encapsulated structures usually require simultaneous polymerization of fluorescent centres, template molecules, functional monomers and cross linkers in a solvent to form a polymer, after which the templates are washed away to obtain the fluorescent imprinted microspheres (He et al., 2023; Meng et al., 2024). The fluorescent centres and functional monomers are uniformly embedded in the fluorescence-imprinted microspheres, so that most of the imprinted sites of the MIPs prepared by this method are distributed within the polymer. The fluorescent centres are usually embedded in the fluorescent imprinted microspheres in two ways: the luminescent elements may be directly embedded in the fluorescent imprinted microspheres (He et al., 2024) or the luminescent elements are embedded in entire COFs/MOFs embedded in the fluorescent imprinted microspheres (Wang et al., 2023b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent elements are embedded directly into MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: Direct embedding of luminescent elements into MIPs is achieved by adding the luminescent elements directly into the reaction, with constant stirring to ensure that they are uniformly dispersed in the imprinted microspheres. Currently used luminescent elements typically include photoluminescent nanoparticles, which include quantum dots (QDs) (Ren et al., 2015), carbon dots (CDs) (Feng et al., 2018), peptide dots (PDs) (Han et al., 2023), metal nanoparticles (MNs) (Wang et al., 2022b), metal organic frameworks (MOFs) (Sahoo et al., 2022) and covalent organic frameworks (COFs) (He et al., 2024).

QDs are nanoscale semiconductor particles, usually composed of II-VI, IV-VI, or III-V elements in the periodic table (Alivisatos, 1996; Nirmal & Brus, 1999; Schmid, 1992; Xu et al., 2018). QDs have unique optical characteristics, such as high quantum yields and long-term stabilities. QDs have broad and continuous absorption pectra, and their UV–visible absorption bands usually start from the emission peak position and increase steadily toward the UV, resulting in QDs with wide excitation peaks; additionally, their excitation wavelengths usually start from any wavelength below the emission peak position. Therefore, it is possible to use an excitation source with the same wavelength to excite many different QDs, thus enabling multichannel detection. In addition to the long fluorescence lifetime and resistance to photobleaching, which increase the uses of QDs, the emission wavelengths of QDs can vary according to their sizes. Therefore, compared with those of other fluorescent materials, the unique optical properties of QDs have obvious advantages in fluorescence detection and biosensing (Karakoti et al., 2015; Li et al., 2022a; Mantashloo & Bahar, 2023). However, the potential toxicity, surface properties and water solubilities of QDs limit widespread application. During long-term exposure of QDs to air, surface oxidation may occur, leading to exposure of the metal ions from the QDs, which may result in metal toxicity. Therefore, stable compounds are needed to cover the surfaces of QDs used for biological applications to prevent surface functionalization. For example, to reduce the toxicities of QDs, Chmangui et al. (2019) chose Mn2+-doped ZnS QDs as the fluorescence center and modified their surface with polyethylene glycol (PEG) to increase the water solubility. Subsequently, 5,7-dimethoxy-coumarin (DMC) was used as a virtual template, methacrylic acid (MAA) was used as a functional monomer, and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was used as a pore-inducing agent to synthesize MIPs for the detection of aflatoxins (AFs) in nondairy products (Chmangui et al., 2019).

Compared with semiconductor QDs, CDs are easy to synthesize and have excellent physical and optical properties, such as low toxicities, good water solubilities, biocompatibilities, fluorescence tunabilities and abundant surface functional groups (Li et al., 2022b; Luo et al., 2020a; Sobiech et al., 2021). Since the low quantum yields of CDs limit application in fluorescence sensing, nonmetal doping of CDs is an effective strategy to improve fluorescence. Heteroatomic doping has a significant effect on the fluorescence properties of the CDs and changes their electronic properties by introducing additional active sites (Huang et al., 2023; Li et al., 2022b). For example, Hu et al. (2021) prepared strongly blue fluorescent Mg and N codoped CDs exhibiting a quantum yield of 33.05 %, which was much greater than that of N-doped CDs. Using CDs as the fluorescence centres, supersensitive MIPs were prepared for the detection of harmful substances in animal foods (Fig. 2 a). The fluorescence response of the synthesized MIPs was significantly greater than those of other synthesized NIPs exhibiting detection ranges of 5–100 ng/mL for tetracycline. In addition, the imprinted factor of the MIPs was 3.76, indicating that the MIPs had good specificity for the recognition of the target (Hu et al., 2021). Compared with CDs synthesized from commercial chemical reagents, biomass CDs have low toxicities and ecological and environmental friendliness. Moreover, the raw materials for biomass CDs are easy to obtain, inexpensive and abundant and can be obtained directly from natural foods. Therefore, researchers are actively exploring the exploitation of biomass CDs (Yang et al., 2022). Currently, the syntheses of CDs from biological wastes, such as orange peels (Wang et al., 2020a), banana peels (Atchudan et al., 2021), corn stalks (Wang et al., 2022a), green tea residues (Hu et al., 2020a) and sweet potato peels (Liu et al., 2019a), have been reported. In addition, Han et al. (2022a) synthesized blue-emitting biomass CDs with discarded watermelon rinds as the raw material, and the resulting quantum yield was 22.8 %. Using CDs as the fluorescent centres, MIPs were synthesized by bulk polymerization for detection of ethyl carbamate (EC), a harmful substance in alcohol. The detection results showed that the synthesized MIPs had high sensitivity and excellent recognition ability (Han et al., 2022a). The reuse of biomass waste provides new ideas for green and sustainable development. However, most of the biomass CDs found thus far emit blue or green light, and fewer biomass CDs emit longer wavelengths, such as red fluorescence, which leads to susceptibility of the synthesized biomass CDs to interference from background light. In addition, the low fluorescence intensities of these materials limit further application of the biomass CDs for detection. Therefore further research is needed to improve the sensitivities and accuracies of biomass CDs for target detection.

As a novel biobased self-assembled nanostructure, PDs have broader biocompatibilities than CDs and inorganic QDs. Due to their simple structures, controlled assembly, biodegradabilities, tunable and bright fluorescence, PDs are of wide interest for fluorescence sensing (Ji et al., 2021; Zhu et al., 2023a). Zhu et al. (2023a) synthesized PDs with emission peaks at 515 nm and 650 nm via self-assembly of C-terminal amidated diphenylalanine and genipin (Fig. 2b). The PDs exhibited controllable particle sizes and excellent fluorescence properties, and showed good detection sensitivities for the targets (Zhu et al., 2023a). Han et al. synthesized luminescent elements from the diphenylalanine dipeptide and used them as a centrepiece to synthesized MIPs with dual signal response, which eliminated background interference while improving the efficiency of fluorescence detection (Fig. 2c). The MIPs had a wide detection range and a low detection limit for the target EC and supported visual detection of the target EC by converting the colours of the sensors to RGB values with a smartphone (Han et al., 2023). The combination of PDs with molecular imprinting technology improved their ability to specifically erecognize the target and further improved the accuracy of detection.

MNs selfassemble from small atoms, have excellent fluorescence stabilities and biocompatibilities, and are widely used in bioimaging and sensors due to their simple syntheses and low toxicities (Duan et al., 2022; Xu et al., 2023). The most commonly used MNs include gold nanoparticles, silver nanoparticles, copper nanoparticles and nickel nanoparticles. Induced aggregation emission of gold nanoparticles results in strong fluorescence intensities. In addition, the small sizes of MNs, their fluorescence stabilities and resistance to photobleaching have attracted widespread interest (Wu et al., 2015). Copper nanoparticles have optical properties similar to those of precious MNs, and the precursors are less expensive and more abundant (Jayasree et al., 2018). Wang et al. (2022b) synthesized an electrochemiluminescent sensor for the detection of enrofloxacin (ENR) by using 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) functionalized copper nanoclusters as the luminescent elements and o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) as the functional monomers. The sensor exhibited strong and stable chemiluminescence, which enabled the trace detection of ENR in water and biological samples, and provided an effective way to detect bactericides in the environment (Wang et al., 2022b). Due to their high conductivities and good optical properties, MNs are widely used in sensors and other applications.

MOFs are porous coordination polymers formed by coordination of metal ions with organic ligands. They are highly crystalline materials with periodic network structures. Compared with other porous materials, MOFs have high crystallinities, high porosities large specific surface areas and flexible structures. Moreover, the coordination bonds formed between MOFs are stronger than hydrogen bonds, π-π packing and van der Waals forces, so the structures formed by MOFs are more stable (Jiao et al., 2019; Li et al., 2016; Xiao et al., 2023). In addition, MOFs also exhibit excellent adsorption and luminescence properties, and their fluorescence properties can be altered by adjusting the interactions between different components; thus, MOFs are widely used in fluorescence sensing. Amiripour et al. (2021) developed MIPs coated on a glowing zirconium metal–organic skeleton for the detection of chloramphenicol residues in milk and honey (Fig. 2d). The bonded zirconium metal–organic frameworks combined with molecular imprinting provided strong luminescence, a larger specific surface area, and more binding sites to increase the selectivity and sensitivity. Moreover, the synthesized MIP/Zr-LMOFs exhibited excellent repeatability and stability and are promising sensing materials for food hazard detection (Amiripour et al., 2021). The luminescent groups of fluorescent MOFs can be either the metal centres or the ligands, and charge transfer between the metal and the ligand also gives rise to luminescence (Chen et al., 2020; Jie et al., 2023). Most luminous MOFs are composed of lanthanide and transition elements. Lanthanide-doped MOFs have fixed photoluminescence effects, long fluorescence lifetimes, high photon yields and sharp emission bands. Lanthanide-doped MOFs tend to bind oxygen more than traditional transition metal elements, making them more desirable for fluorescence sensing (Sahoo et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2017).

COFs are new crystalline porous materials formed by covalent bonding of fully designed organic monomers. Since most COFs are composed of light elements such as C/N/O/H/P/Si, the resulting structures are more stable. COFs have good physical and chemical stabilities, and large specific surface areas, low densities and high adjustable porosities (Cui et al., 2023; Guo et al., 2021a; Kou et al., 2021). COFs are periodic porous polymers with many similar adsorption sites on their surfaces, which increases their mass transfer efficiencies as well as their detection sensitivities. The large specific surface areas of 2D COFs also enable stronger interactions with the targets. In addition, compared with MOFs, COFs do not contain metal ions and have higher stabilities and biocompatibilities (Hu et al., 2019). Therefore, COFs have been widely studided for use in fluorescence sensors. Han et al. (2022b) prepared a blue fluorescent carbazole-based COF from the monomers 3,6-diaminocarbazole and 2,4,6-trihydroxy-benzene-1,3,5-tricarbaldehyde and encapsulated this COF in MIPs for the detection of toxic and hazardous substances in wine. Carbazole COF-based MIPs used for the detection of ethyl carbamate are highly sensitive, selective and reproducible, making them excellent fluorescent sensing materials (Han et al., 2022b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent elements embedded indirectly into MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent elements such as QDs and CDs have desirable fluorescence properties, high quantum yields, and excellent fluorescence stabilities. However, molecular imprinting with CDs and QDs results in small specific surface areas few adsorption sites, and low sensitivities (Yuan et al., 2021b). When COFs, MOFs and other porous materials combine with the target object, there are many adsorption sites and large surface areas, which enable full interaction with the target object (Liu et al., 2018b). Therefore, the combination of light-emitting elements and porous imprinted materials enhances the fluorescence properties, improves the detection sensitivity and increase the number of adsorption sites, thereby improving the adsorption capacity and detection efficiency (Yan et al., 2022). The combination of synthetic fluorescent materials with the molecular imprinting technique (MIT) has led to improved selectivities detected materials and increased the use of fluorescence sensing. For example, Guo et al. (2021b) constructed multifunctional nanoprobes by embedding blue fluorescent CDs synthesized from black wolfberry into Eu-MOFs. The synthesized nanoprobes inherited the fluorescence properties of the CDs and Eu3+. With the addition of Hg2+, blue fluorescence gradually bursts, resulting in gradual changes in the colours of the nanoprobes from blue to red to enable visual detection of Hg2+ in water with high sensitivity (Fig. 2e and f) (Guo et al., 2021b). However, the selectivity of this fluorescent probe must be improved; Xu et al. (2019) synthesized MIPs by embedding blue fluorescent CDs into MOFs and used the CDs@MOF as the fluorescence centres (Fig. 2g). The detection limit for the target object was 0.50 nM, and the linear range was 0–2.9 μM. The CDs@ZIF-8@MIP nanoparticles combined the advantages of CDs, MOFs and MIPs, and exhibited high fluorescence intensity, good fluorescence stability, strong adsorption and short adsorption equilibrium times. The specific surface area of the CDs@ZIF-8@MIP was 879.43 m2/g, which was significantly greater than that of CDs@MIP (123.05 m2/g). Moreover, it had good selectivity and high sensitivity for the target, and has been successfully uesd for the detection of quercetin (QCT) in healthy food (Xu et al., 2019).

The syntheses of MIPs formed by traditional embedding structures are simple. Functional monomers and fluorescent elements are dispersed within the MIPs, but the dispersion depths of imprinted sites are uneven, which leads to difficult template elution, mass transfer resistance, and difficult adsorption of target objects (Niu et al., 2016; Wan et al., 2017). Therefore, to overcome the shortcomings of traditional MIPs, researchers have developed new synthetic strategies. MIPs with core–shell structures were synthesized by sol–gel, precipitation polymerization, emulsion polymerization, suspension polymerization and surface imprinting techniques. The functional monomers and fluorescent elements of the core–shell MIPs are distributed on or near the imprinted surface, so the template molecules are easier to remove, more sensitive, and more selective, and they have faster mass transfer rates; thus, these materials are widely used in sensors. Current studies of core–shell structure MIPs can be divided into single-emission fluorescence, double-emission fluorescence and multiemission fluorescence according to the fluorescing elements.

Generally, MIPs with core–shell structures are composed of a core and an MIP shell. The core supports the MIPs, which form an imprinted layer on the surface for target identification and adsorption (Bhogal et al., 2020). In addition to the abovementioned fluorescent elements, the core materials have also included SiO2-coated fluorescent elements and fluorescent elements covering on the surfaces of magnetic nanoparticles (Niu et al., 2016).

SiO2 is widely used because of its high stability, biocompatibility and high permeability of template molecules. In addition, SiO2 is phototransparent and chemically inert, which is effective for protecting the fluorescent nanomaterials (Niu et al., 2016; Sun et al., 2008). Therefore, fluorescent elements embedded within SiO2 are often used as fluorescence centres for MIPs. Qiu et al. (2015) achieved surface imprinting by preparing SiO2-coated CdTe QDs as fluorescence centres via the sol–gel method for the detection of trace BPA in water and milk. The fluorescence intensity of the CdTe QDs significantly increased with increasing concentrations of BPA, and the linear range for BPA detection with the MIPs was 0.05–10 μmol/L, with a detection limit of 6 nmol/L. The synthesized MIPs exhibit good stabilities and reusabilities, and wide application prospects for food detection (Qiu et al., 2015). Kazemifard et al. (2020) used rosemary as a raw material to synthesize environmentally friendly biomass CDs, which reduced the use of harmful chemicals, and fixed SiO2 shells on the surfaces of the CDs via reverse-phase microemulsion technology. A fluorescence sensor was synthesized for detection of thiabendazole (TBZ) residues in fruit juice, and CDs@SiO2 was used as the fluorescent core. The sensor exhibited environmental friendliness, simple synthetic methods and high sensitivity (8 ng/mL), and provided rapid and accurate detection of TBZ (Kazemifard et al., 2020).

Magnetic nanoparticles are often used in sensors and adsorbents because of their large specific surface areas and strong adsorption capacities. Because magnetic nanomaterials can be separated from solution with an external magnetic field, harmful substances can be separated efficiently and quickly (Mantashloo & Bahar, 2023; Niu et al., 2016). Therefore, combinations with MIT increased the selectivity and sensitivity, allowing accurate, rapid detection and adsorption of target objects. With room temperature phosphorescent QDs used as luminescent materials and Fe3O4 nanoparticles as the core, Chen et al. (2021) developed a novel molecularly imprinted sensor (MIS) through surface MIT for efficient and rapid detection of norfloxacin (NFX) residues in food. The MIS exhibited phosphorescence at room temperature, a low detection limit (0.80 μg/L) and a wide detection range (1–90 μg/L), and was used for the detection of trace NFX in milk. Because of the presence of magnetic nanoparticles, it is advantageous to reuse the fluorescent sensors (Chen et al., 2021). Wang et al. (2022c) introduced a magnetic COF to construct fluorescence sensors for the first time. First, a magnetic COF was prepared by in-situ production of the COF on Fe3O4 nanoparticles, which were used as the magnetic centres of MIPs. Then, a CD@MIP film was deposited on the surface of the magnetic COF via the sol–gel method. Finally, MIPs that adsorbed the target BPA were obtained. The synthesized MIPs had the magnetic properties of Fe3O4, the fluorescence properties of CDs, and the large specific surface area and adsorption sites of COFs and provided precise recognition of template molecules, which were used for the detection of hazardous substances in complex foods, indicating that the MIPs were accurate, reliable and practical fluorescence sensors (Wang et al., 2022c).

Most reported fluorescence sensors rely on single-emission fluorescent elements, but single emission fluorescent elements are easily affected by probes, instruments and the environment. In contrast to single-fluorescence emission, double fluorescence emission refers to two different fluorescence emission wavelengths induced with the same excitation source. Dual-fluorescence emission sensors have built-in self-calibration functions and eliminate the adverse effects caused by external factors, such as background interference and instrument fluctuations; therefore, they have higher sensitivities and accuracies in detecting trace analytes. In addition, double-fluorescence emission probes exhibit more obvious colour changes during detection than single-fluorescen probes and provide great advantages for use in visual fluorescence probes (Cui et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2020b; Lian et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2022c). At present, dual emission fluorescence probes exhibit ratiometric fluorescence. Wang et al. (2020b) used blue graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as the response signals and red CdTe quantum dots (CdTe QDs) as reference signals. First, the red CdTe QDs were encapsulated inside SiO2 with the sol–gel method, and then the imprinted layers with blue GQDs were encapsulated on the SiO2 surfaces by precipitation polymerization. After the target molecules were washed from the surface, MIPs with a core–shell structure were obtained for detecting oxytetracycline (OTC) (Fig. 3 a). As the concentration of the target increased, the fluorescence intensity of the signal at 435 nm decreased gradually, while the fluorescence intensity of the signal at 660 nm was almost unchanged. The ratio of the two fluorescence intensities was linearly related to the concentration of the target, and the colours of the MIPs gradually changed from blue to pink, enabling sensitive visual detection of hygromycin in milk (Wang et al., 2020b). Hu et al. (2022) encapsulated Mg with a blue response signal and N-CDs into Eu-MOFs the emitting the red reference signal, and synthesized proportional dual-emission Mg, N-CDs/Eu-MOFs via self-assembly. With 3-amino-propyl triethoxy silane (APTES) as the functional monomer and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) as the crosslinking agent, ratiometric MIPs were synthesized by the sol–gel method for detection of OTC. In the presence of OTC, the blue fluorescence (Mg, N-CDs) at 445 nm was quenched due to the internal filtering effect (IFE), while the red fluorescence (Eu-MOFs) at 620 nm was significantly enhanced due to the antenna effect (AE), and the colour clearly changed from blue to red. The construction of dual-emission fluorescence elements reduced interference and provided signal amplification via a synergistic combination of the internal filter effect (IFE) and antenna effect (AE), which improved the detection sensitivity of the target (Hu et al., 2022).

In multi-emission fluorescent sensors three or more emission wavelengths are activated with the same excitation wavelength. When the sensors were constructed with multiple emission signals, the intensities of the multiple emission signals increased or decreased different degrees to reduce the interference from external factors and enhance the sensitivities and accuracies of the sensors. The colour changes in dual-emission fluorescence sensors are attributed to a transition colour between two colorscolours, but these three-emission fluorescence sensors increase the range of fluorescence colour changes and increase the degree of colour changes in the spectrum, so that multicolour fluorescence sensors can be applied more broadly for visual detection (You et al., 2022; Zhu et al., 2021). For example, You et al. (2022) prepared blue, green and red fluorescent sensors for visual detection of dibutyl phthalate in fish samples (Fig. 3b). First, the ratio of the fluorescent QDs emitting the three colours was optimized to determine the maximum colour richness. The final version included 8 %, 10 %, and 8 % MIP-blue-quantum dots, GQD, and MIP-red-quantum dots respectively, in the sensors. As the concentration of dibutyl phthalate (DBP) increased, the intensities of the blue fluorescence at 450 nm and red fluorescence at 630 nm gradually decreased, while the intensity of the green fluorescence at 530 nm did not change. Finally, the colour of the sensors changed to plum, pink, oolitic, yellowish green, and finally green. Moreover, the RGB values of the fluorescence sensors showed good linear relationship with the concentration of the target (0.002–20 mg/L), and the detection limit of the fluorescence sensors for the target in fish samples was as low as 1 μg/L; thus, these sensors could be used for rapid detection of DBP in food. The integration of different luminescent MIP-QDs into sensors produced rich fluorescence colour changes and improved the detection sensitivity, accuracy and efficiency, which will be important for the development of fluorescent sensors in the future (You et al., 2022).

The recognition sites of solid core–shell structure MIPs are distributed in the interiors of the nanospheres in addition to the surface, which limits mass transfer and the adsorption sensitivity. Hollow imprinted microspheres were synthesized by removing both the core and the template molecules from the MIPs, exposing the imprinted sites on the surface as well as the inner surface, and increasing the area for interaction between the target and the imprinted sites. Hollow imprinted microspheres showed lower mass transfer resistance, improved the binding efficiency and enhanced the detection sensitivity (Wang et al., 2019). Li et al. (2018) synthesized MIPs with the sol–gel method by using carboxyl functionalized polystyrene as the core and N, S codoped CDs as the luminescent elements, finally, methylene chloride and acetone were used to remove the polystyrene particles and template molecules to obtain hollow shell MIPs. Hollow shell MIPs were used for detection of tetracycline (TC) in honey (Fig. 3c). The time required to synthesize hollow shell MIPs was greatly shortened. Compared with traditional core–shell MIPs, they exhibited larger comparative area, shorter adsorption time, and significantly improved sensitivities, laying the foundation for subsequent development of fluorescent hollow shell MIPs (Li et al., 2018).

Mesoporous MIPs have large surface areas, low mass transfer resistances, strong binding with the target, and high sensitivities and detection efficiencies. Moreover, the fluorescent material embedded in the mesoporous sensor reduced the self-quenching caused by aggregation of the fluorescent material, improved the fluorescence performance, and increased the sensitivity of the fluorescent sensor (Xu & Lu, 2016; Xu et al., 2022; Zhou et al., 2016). Mesoporous SiO2 has received extensive attention for use in nanotechnology and fluorescence sensing because of its orderly structure, adjustable pore sizes, large specific surface areas, strong stability, and good physical and chemical properties (Bhogal et al., 2022; Jia et al., 2017; Susanti & Hasanah, 2021). Xu et al. (2022) synthesized oxytetracycin-based (OTC) mesoporous fluorescent MIPs by using N-CDs as the fluorescent material and cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) as the poreforming agent. Compared with those of nonmesoporous MIPs, the sensitivities and response times of structured MIPs in target detection were increased by 1.4 times and reduced by 9 times, respectively, facilitating faster and more sensitive detection of terramycin, and successfully applied to the detection and analysis of terramycin in milk and honey (Xu et al., 2022). Jalili et al. (2020) prepared MIPs with mesoporous structures via the sol–gel method and used double-emission carbon dots as the fluorescent centres and CTAB as the mesoporous structural template, and the mesoporous fluorescent MIPs were subsequently used for detection of penicillin G in food samples (Fig. 3d). The experimental results showed that the specific surface areas of the MIPs with mesoporous structures (1089.12 m2/g) were much greater than those of MIPs with nonmesoporous structures (27.13 m2/g). In contrast, the detection limits and response times of the mesoporous MIPs were considerably lower than those of the other materials, suggesting that the mesoporous structure reduced mass transfer resistance between the MIPs and the target and improved the binding capacity between them. Therefore, mesoporous MIPs constructed with ratio fluorescence showed excellent visual detection capability and rapid detection; additionally, these materials can detect harmful substances in complex food mixtures, laying a foundation for rapid and portable detection in the future (Jalili et al., 2020).

Hollow mesoporous MIPs, which have larger specific surface areas than hollow shell MIPs and mesoporous MIPs, have more imprinting sites on their inner and outer surfaces. The hollow structures, adjustable void sizes, and large specific surface areas of hollow mesoporous MIPs provided significant advantages for adsorption and detection (Bhogal et al., 2021). For example, Lu and Xu (2017) prepared hollow mesoporous MIPs by chemical etching for the detection of oestrogens in food (Fig. 3e). The synthesized hollow mesoporous MIPs showed excellent mass transfer and recognition capabilities, which improved the detection efficiency (Lu & Xu, 2017). Zhao et al. (2012) prepared chemiluminescent hollow mesoporous MIPs with mesoporous SiO2 as the core. First, APETS and acryloyl chloride were used to modify the surface of the mesoporous silica particles, after which MIPs were synthesized by precipitation polymerization. After the SiO2 core was dissolved with hydrofluoric acid, hollow mesoporous MIPs were obtained. The synthesized hollow mesoporous MIPs contained high-density binding sites on both the inner and outer surfaces and quickly and efficiently adsorbed fenpropathrin in food. Moreover, the prepared chemiluminescence sensors had high sensitivities and selectivities and were used for detection of harmful substances in food (Zhao et al., 2012).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visualization of molecularly imprinted polymers for capture of α-DCs and their associated hazards
ce:section.ce:para: The Maillard reaction refers to chemical reactions between amino-containing compounds (proteins and amino acids) and the carbonyl group of reducing sugars, this reaction occurs during food storage and thermal processing, and significantly improves the color and flavours of thermally processed foods (Kathuria et al., 2023). The Maillard reaction consists of three main stages. In the initial stage, the carbonyl group of the reducing sugar and the free amino group form Amadori products through glycosylation. In the intermediate stage, the Amadori product is cleaved to give reactive carbonyl compounds. Low-molecular-weight carbonyl compounds are the main sources of colour, aroma and flavour, and are important precursors to toxic and hazardous substances such as AM, heterocyclic amines, and AGEs. In the final stage, the reactive carbonyl compound undergoes a series of complex reactions to form flavour compounds as well as the Maillard reaction hazards (Dong et al., 2023; Shakoor et al., 2022; Shi et al., 2024). The presence of Maillard reaction hazards not only reduces the nutritional value of the food, but also tends to accumulate in the body, which can lead to the occurrence of chronic diseases such as diabetes, Alzheimer's disease and cardiovascular disease (ALjahdali & Carbonero, 2019). Therefore, accurate and rapid detection of Maillard reaction hazards in food is very important for protecting human health. Currently, the main commonly studied Maillard products include AM, AGEs and α-DCs. Table 1 shows the visual MIPs used for detection of the three common hazards in recent years.

AM is a toxic substance produced by Maillard reactions in food, and studies have shown that AM is immunotoxic, genotoxic, neurotoxic, and carcinogenic. However, AM is widely present in a variety of foods and is potentially harmful to human health; therefore, accurate and rapid detection of AM during the Maillard reaction is very important (Ali & El-Wekil, 2023; Orsák et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2020b). Faraji et al. (2018) first extracted AM from foods via ultrasonic extraction, followed by microextraction after derivatization with 2-naphthol, and finally detected the AM contents in the samples with liquid chromatography. The limit for detection of AM with this method was 3.0 μg/L (Faraji et al., 2018). However, the pretreatment process for AM detection with liquid chromatography is complicated, and the required instrumentation is expensive and cannot provide fast and convenient detection. Therefore, Liu et al. (2016a) constructed molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensors with sol–gel technology, which provided low-cost and rapid detection of AM in potato chips (Liu et al., 2016a). In recent years, visualization detection has received widespread attention from scholars because of its convenience and rapidity. Pattnayak and Mohapatra (2023) detected AM in aqueous solutions with GSHCQD-Au NPs, and the GSHCQDs burst when the Au NPs were added. However, when AM was added, energy transfer between the Au NPs and GSHCQDs was prevented, the fluorescence was restored, and rapid detection of AM in water was achieved within 7 min (Pattnayak & Mohapatra, 2023). In addition, Demirhan et al. (2018) constructed Mn-doped ZnS QD room temperature phosphorescent sensors for detection of AM in food samples; these sensors had unique optical properties, fast detection speeds and low interference from the outside. Optical-based sensors enabled easy and fast detection but lackd selectivity for the target (Demirhan et al., 2018). Therefore, Liu et al. (2017) combined visual sensors with MIT to construct fluorescent MIPs for detection of AM in complex food systems. The fluorescent probes detected AM concentrations in the range of 0.5–60 μmol/L with a detection limit of 0.17 μmol/L. Compared with that of the NIP, the fluorescence response of the MIP was significant, indicating that the MIP had excellent specificity in recognizing AM. The fluorescence MIS achieved high sensitivity and specificity in detection of trace AM in food samples, which provided a simple and fast method for the subsequent detection of AM in food (Liu et al., 2017).

Advanced glycosylation end products (AGEs) are compounds produced irreversibly from reducing sugars, amino acids, lipids, and nucleic acids in the late stages of the Maillard reaction (Chen & Guo, 2021; Liu et al., 2018a). AGEs are mainly divided into endogenous and exogenous AGEs. Endogenous AGEs are generated by reactions in the human body, and exogenous AGEs are ingested with the diet. Foodborne AGEs are not easily excreted, thus, they accumulate in the body and can cause damage to human health (Liu et al., 2018a; Yuan et al., 2021). Numerous studies have shown that the accumulation of AGEs in the body is associated with the development of certain chronic diseases, such as cardiovascular diseases, diabetes and its complications, atherosclerosis, and Alzheimer's disease, as well as human aging and retinopathy. Therefore, the detection and suppression of AGEs in food are highly important (Khan et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2013). Current studies on AGEs have been focused on carboxymethyl lysine (CML), carboxyethyl lysine (CEL) and pyrraline (PRL). Scheijen et al. (2016) performed quantitative analyses of the targets CEL, CML, and Nδ-(5-hydro-5-methyl-4-imidazolon-2-yl)-ornithine (MG-H1) with ultra-high performance liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry and a stable isotope-labeled internal standard, and quantified the concentrations of CEL, CML and MG-H1 in 190 food items (Scheijen et al., 2016). This method enabled simultaneous and accurate quantification of three AGEs, but the method was complicated by pretreatment, time consumption and the need for expensive instruments. Zhu et al. (2020) detected the contents of AGEs in different parts of traditional Chinese red-roasted chickens, including the free and bound states of CEL and CML, with enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (Zhu et al., 2020). The ELISA method is simple and rapid and has been used for detection of AGEs in many foods. However, specific antibodies are need for detection of AGEs, and AGE detection is susceptible to the lipid matrixes in foods, which may lead to biased results (Yuan et al., 2021). Liu et al. (2016b) constructed an optical MIS to achieve highly selective visual detection of CML. The group used hydrophobic CdSe/ZnS QDs as the luminescence centres and APTES as the functional monomer to synthesize optical sensing materials with high dispersion and good compatibility via the reverse microemulsion method. Highly efficient and rapid detection of CML was achieved (Liu et al., 2016b). Subsequently, the group introduced MOFs into MIPs to improve the optical and adsorption performance of the sensors, increased the sensitivity of the sensor with dual signal amplification, and detect of PRL over a wide concentration range (5 × 10−6–1 × 10−3 mol/L). The detection limit was as low as 3.9 × 10−6 mol/L, enabling efficient and rapid detection of PRL in milk powder (Liu et al., 2018b). The visualized fluorescent sensing materials were inexpensive, simple to use and rapid, and they were highly sensitive and selective and important for the detection of AGEs in foods.

AGEs are ingested mainly from the diet and are easily enriched in the body, therefore, it is crucial to control the production of AGEs in the food system. Current research on AGEs inhibition have been focused mainly on plant extracts; such as Yu et al. (2017) investigated the inhibitory effects of sugarcane extract on CML and CEL (Yu et al., 2017). Their results showed that the sugarcane molasses extract contained many phenolic compounds that powerful antioxidanst and thus inhibited the generation of CML and CEL in the simulated system. However, this method required a complex process to extract the phenolic compounds, and those obtained by extraction cannot be reused. Therefore, Zhu et al. (2023b) prepared FMIPs encapsulating AVA 2f by reverse microemulsion with oat bran-derived CDs as fluorescent element for blocking AGEs in food matrices (Zhu et al., 2023b). Their results showed that FMIP inhibited the AGEs by releasing AVA 2f into the food. The efficiency for FMIP inhibition of PRL in milk was as high as 44.91 % at 60 °C, which provided precise blocking of PRL at high temperatures. This method was simple and rapid, and provided AGEs inhibition at high temperatures. In addition, Yuan et al. (2023a) combined magnetically controlled nanoparticles with an optical sensing platform to recognize and adsorb CML and removed it from the food system with an external magnetic field (Fig. 4 a) (Yuan et al., 2023a). The nanoparticles showed a high CML removal efficiency of 89.6 %. This material enabled rapid and accurate capture of CML in food matrices, resisted interference and was reusable, which is very important for the removal of AGEs from complex food matrices.

Alpha-dicarbonyl compounds (α-DCs), including glyoxal (GO), methylglyoxal (MGO), and diacetyl (DA), are precursors to heterocyclic flavour chemicals and are also important intermediates in the formation of AGEs. Increases in the number of α-DCs causes the formation of AGEs, which may lead to the risk of chronic diseases such as Alzheimer's disease, cardiovascular disease and diabetes (Gürsul & Gökmen, 2020; Qi et al., 2023). The current methods used in detecting α-DCs involve liquid chromatography and gas chromatography. Wang et al. (2018) used 4-(2,3-dimethyl-6-quinoxalinyl)-1,2-phenylenediamine as the derivatization reagent and achieved derivatization under mild conditions, and five α-DCs (3-DG, Gly, MGly, DA, and PD) were detected in white wine and beer via HPLC-UV (Wang et al., 2018). Lim and Shin (2020) simultaneously determined GO and MGO in alcohol and fermented foods with headspace solid-phase microextraction and gas chromatography–mass spectrometry; the volatile 2,2,2-trifluoroethylhydrazine (TFEH) was used as the derivatization reagent in measuring GO and MGO simultaneously with HS-SPME-GC-MS. This method had a low detection limit and measured the two targets simultaneously, which provided a new ideas for measurements of α-DCs in alcohol and fermented foods (Lim & Shin, 2020). Since α-DCs do not absorb ultraviolet light, derivatization reactions are required for detection with HPLC or GC; however, these reactions are complicated, and may involve incomplete derivatization.

However, fluorescent MITs can avoid these problems, and MIPs provided rapid recognition and adsorption by α-DCs with excellent sensitivities and selectivities, and achieved specific adsorption of the target in a short time. In addition to recognition of the α-DCs, MIPs generated specific adsorption, which in turn led to inhibition of their end products. He et al. (2023a) synthesized thermosensitive MIPs with fluorescent AGEs-derived CDs used as the luminescent centres for recognizing and trapping 3-DG in milk samples at high temperatures (He et al., 2023a). The linear range for 3-DG recognition by the synthesized MIPs was 1–160 μg/L, and the detection limit was 0.31 μg/L. Fluorescent MIT enabled rapid recognition and capture of 3-DG in complex food mechanisms with excellent selectivity and interference resistance. The synthesized heat-sensitive MIPs inhibited expression of the end product PRL by adsorbing 3-DG. The results showed that the PRL inhibition rate of the MIPs was 23 % in milk, which indicated that AGEs could be inhibited by adsorption of the intermediate α-DCs. Yuan et al. (2023b) achieved real-time monitoring as well as capture of α-DCs by combining peptide nanodots with magnetic nanoparticles (Fig. 4b) (Yuan et al., 2023b). Rapid adsorption (10 min) of GO, MGO and 3-DG was achieved by constructing SPNPs@mSiO2@B-β-AH nanoparticles to inhibit degradation of the end products AGEs. The results showed that the percentages of PYR, CML and CEL inhibited by SPNPs@mSiO2@B-β-AH were 66.9 %, 31.0 % and 41.4 %, respectively. Moreover, the incorporation of magnetic nanoparticles contributed to rapid removal of the targets from the food matrix, preventing the incorporation of new hazardous substances and risk. This method confirmed that the AGEs can be inhibited by adsorption of the α-DCs, which provides a new idea for improving dietary health and industrial development.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual recognition mechanisms for Maillard reaction hazards with MIPs
ce:section.ce:para: When the target binds to a fluorescent element, the fluorescent element bursts. Current burst mechanisms include static quenching (SQE), dynamic quenching (DQE), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and the inner filter effect (IFE) (Luo et al., 2020a). A diagram of the different burst mechanisms is shown in Fig. 5 .

SQE refers to the formation of a nonfluorescent compound after a chemical reaction between the fluorescent element and the target, thus leading to a reduction in the fluorescence intensity. When SQE occurs, the fluorescence lifetime of the fluorescent element hardly changes; new compounds are generated and cause the UV absorption peak to change; as the temperature increases, the stability of the new compound decreases, which leads to a decrease in the burst effect (Liu et al., 2019b, 2020a). Liu et al. (2019a) prepared biomass CDs from sweet potato peels, and when the target hygromycin bound to the CDs, a new UV absorption peak was generated at 357 nm; the fluorescence lifetime of the CDs did not significantly change, indicating that the interaction between OTC and the CDs led to SQE (Liu et al., 2019a).

DQE involves return of CDs to the ground state after collisions between the excited CDs and the burster. Unlike the case for SQE, no new compounds are produced during DQE; therefore, the UV absorption peak is not significantly altered. In addition, the fluorescence lifetime of the CDs is not affected by the bursting agent, and the bursting efficiency of the CDs increases depending on with increases in the temperature (Zu et al., 2017). Xu et al. (2022) synthesized N-doped CDs from citric acid and urea and used the CDs as fluorescent centres to synthesize MIPs. When the N-CDs were combined with OTC, the positions of their UV absorption peaks did not change significantly. In addition, the emission peaks of the N-CDs did not overlap with the UV absorption peaks of OTC. Therefore, the interaction between OTC and the N-CDs identified as DQE (Xu et al., 2022).

PET indicates that electron transfer occurred between the CDs and the burster, forming cationic and anionic radicals. PET includes both oxidized PET and reduced PET, with oxidized PET driven by the energy gap between the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of the CDs and the LOMO of the target. In reduced PET, the CDs serve as electron acceptors and accept electrons from the target, and this process is driven by the energy gap between the LUMO of the target and the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of the CDs (Zu et al., 2017). Therefore, the fluorescence lifetime of the CDs is not changed by PET, and the UV absorption spectrum of the CDs does not change after addition of the target. In addition, the UV absorption peaks of the targets are far from the emission peaks of the CDs. For example, Luo et al. (2020b) synthesized MIPs for detection of HMF in honey and used CaF2 as QDs. When HMF was added, electron transfer from the MIPs to the LUMO of HMF resulted in a fluorescence burst of the MIPs (Luo et al., 2020b).

FRET occurs between the excited state of the fluorescent element and the ground state of the target. The following conditions must be met for FRET to occur: the emission spectrum of the fluorescent element coincides with the absorption spectrum of the target, the distance between the fluorescent element and the target is in the range of 10–100 Å, and the fluorescence lifetime of the light emitting element changes when the target is added (Chen et al., 2022; Zu et al., 2017). Yang et al. (2019) prepared ratiometric MIPs with CdTe QDs and ZnS QDs; the CdTe QDs had an emission peak at 630 nm, which coincided with the UV absorption spectrum of the targeted bright blue. When the CdTe QDs were spatially close to the targeted bright blue, the energy generated by the transfer of excited-state electrons from the CdTe QDs to the ground state was transferred to the target bright blue, and the electrons on the bright blue to change from the ground state to the excited state (Yang et al., 2019).

IFE refers to a fluorescence burst caused by absorption or emission of light by substances other than fluorescent elements and the target in the reaction system. Unlike FRET and PET, IFE does not have a distance limit between the fluorescent element and the target (Luo et al., 2020a; Wang et al., 2017). However, IFE still requires that the emission spectrum of the fluorescent elements overlaps with the UV absorption spectrum of the target. Since no new material is produced by the IFE process, no new UV absorption spectra are produced and the fluorescence lifetime of the fluorescent elements does not change (Liu et al., 2019b). He et al. (2023b) used all-inorganic caesium lead halides (CsPbX3, X = Cl, Br, I) perovskite quantum dots (PQDs) as the fluorescent centres of MIPs to detect Sudan I in foodstuffs. The UV absorption spectra of the CsPbBr3 PQDs overlapped with that of Sudan I. The absence of new peaks in the UV absorption spectra of the CsPbBr3 PQDs after the addition of Sudan I indicated that there was no interaction between the CsPbBr3 PQDs and Sudan I, which was consistent with the observ ations for IFE (He et al., 2023b).

In addition to fluorescence bursts, fluorescence enhancement may also result from binding of the target to the fluorescent element. The mechanisms for fluorescence enhancement that have been investigated include metal-enhanced fluorescence (MEF), the antenna effect (AE) and aggregation-induced enhancement (AIE). For example, Gao et al. (2015) synthesized fluorescence-enhanced CDs for detection of metallic Ag. The fluorescence of the CDs was enhanced when they interacted with Ag nanoclusters, resulting in the detection of Ag (Gao et al., 2015). Hu et al. (2022) prepared a dual-emission fluorescent MIS; when the target OTA was added, the MIS formed a metal chelate with Eu3+ and transferred the absorbed energy to Eu3+ via AE, resulting in enhanced fluorescence at 620 nm (Hu et al., 2022).

Visual MIPs integrate signal response elements into molecular recognition monomers, with good selectivities, reusabilities, and portabilities. They can be customized and adjusted with different polymerization mechanisms and methods to control the adsorption and desorption of target analytes. Although these visual MIPs, which have outstanding advantages, have provided significant breakthroughs in identifying recognition targets, they still face formidable challenges in the detection and inhibition of α-dicarbonyl compound-mediated Maillard reactions. In the process of food thermo-processing, the complexities of food ingredients and complex chemical reactions, such as the Maillard reaction, can lead to diverse forms of ingredients in food, posing new difficulties for precise control of the hazards. Therefore, controlling the intermediate products of chemical reactions with visual MIPs to the generation of hazards is a cutting-edge application of this technology.

Visual MIPs also face challenges in practical applications. The main steps include following: (1) Visual MIPs with high target-specificity formed with novel imprinting techniques yielding porous MIPs featuring rapid processes, high capacities and high affinities are urgently needed recognition and inhibition with real samples, as are MIPs for simultaneous recognition of multiple targets. (2) The safety and biocompatibilities of visual MIPs are the biggest obstacles to their in controlling in thermoprocessing. Therefore, suitable forms of MIPs, such as solids or liquid films, magnetic MIPs, and other states that can be removed in a timely manner during processing, must be considered. Green preparations of visual MIPs are also important, such as selecting carbon or peptide dots with low toxicities as the fluorescent response elements and using green reagents to synthesize the MIPs. (3) Industrial scale production of visual MIPs requires proportional adjustment based on laboratory research and development, and the processes often leads to changes in the performance of the MIPs. Moreover, it is also necessary to address the issues of high-sensitivity responses and yields of visual MIPs, such as by selecting fluorescent nanomaterials with high quantum yields and long-wavelength luminescence to avoid background interference. In addition, in the context of food thermal-processing, it is necessary to add temperature sensitive or pH-sensitive elements to adjust the MIPs and adapt to changes in the detection environment.

This review provides insights for the development and application of visual MIPs in recognition and inhibition of α-dicarbonyl compound-mediated Maillard reaction products in real samples. We wish to elaborate on the possibilities of using visual MIPs under special conditions, as well as the practical application potential of visual MIPs. In the future, we hope to continue developing various forms of visual MIPs, as well as portable intelligent reading devices, for full chain applications in food safety traceability, quality component analyses, quality and safety control, and other areas.

Jingbo He: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Lei Wang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Huilin Liu: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Baoguo Sun: Supervision, Conceptualization.
